enrichment.short_description: Bebo is a free social app relaunched by its original founders around small, private group chats called Crews, replacing public feeds and like counts with real-time conversation.
enrichment.seo_title: Bebo in 2026: What The Relaunched Social App Does Now
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bebo is back, run by its founders as a free group chat app built around private Crews. See what Bebo does today and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bebo is a free social networking and group chat app currently owned and operated by its original founders, Michael and Xochi Birch, who first launched Bebo in January 2005 from their home in San Francisco. The original Bebo grew into one of the largest social networks of the mid-2000s, competing with MySpace and Facebook, and was acquired by AOL in March 2008 for approximately 850 million US dollars. AOL sold the struggling network to Criterion Capital Partners in 2010, and the Birches bought Bebo back for around 1 million US dollars in 2013. Since regaining control, the founders have relaunched Bebo multiple times under different concepts, including a 2014 video walkie-talkie app called Blab, and a mobile social and livestreaming app released around 2017 that was shut down by roughly 2019-2020 after failing to gain traction; a subsequent private-beta attempt was also discontinued by 2022. The current version of Bebo, actively developed and re-released in stages through the mid-2020s, abandons the newsfeed, follower counts, and like buttons that defined both the original Bebo and most modern social platforms. Instead, the product is organized around Crews, private group chats of up to roughly 100 people intended for close friend groups rather than broad public audiences. A companion feature called the Spectator Model lets a Crew opt into a semi-public mode, similar to a live, readable podcast, where a core group of up to 50 participants chats while outside spectators can watch without joining the conversation. Bebo is free for individual members, with no subscription fee required to create or join a Crew. Because Bebo has cycled through so many relaunches and shutdowns since 2013, its current user base and market traction are modest compared with the original 2005-2013 network, and the product should be understood as an independent, founder-funded project rather than a mainstream social platform. Gappsy lists Bebo as a case study in social app reinvention: the same brand and founders, an entirely different product built around small-group, real-time conversation rather than public broadcasting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bebo
paragraphs: Bebo is a free social app currently owned and operated by its original founders, Michael and Xochi Birch, who first launched the Bebo social network in January 2005 from their home in San Francisco. The early Bebo grew rapidly through the mid-2000s and became one of the largest social networks of its era, competing directly with MySpace and Facebook before AOL acquired it in March 2008 for roughly 850 million US dollars.

AOL was unable to sustain Bebo's growth, and the network was sold to Criterion Capital Partners in 2010 before the Birches bought it back for around 1 million US dollars in 2013. Since then, the founders have relaunched Bebo multiple times under different concepts, including a 2014 video walkie-talkie app called Blab and a mobile social and livestreaming app released around 2017 that was discontinued by roughly 2019-2020 after limited adoption; a further private-beta attempt was also shut down by 2022.

The current iteration of Bebo, rolled out in stages through the mid-2020s, is a deliberate departure from both the original network and typical modern social apps: it removes public newsfeeds, follower counts, and like buttons, and instead centers on small, private group conversations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bebo's core unit is the Crew, a private group chat of up to roughly 100 people designed for close friend groups rather than broad public audiences. Crews function as real-time chat rooms where members share updates, photos, and conversation without an algorithmic feed sorting or ranking the content.

A companion feature called the Spectator Model lets a Crew opt into a semi-public mode, functioning like a live, readable podcast: a core group of up to 50 participants carries the conversation while outside spectators can watch in real time without being able to join in unless invited.

By removing like counts and other vanity metrics, Bebo aims to reduce the popularity-contest dynamics and algorithmic engagement pressure associated with mainstream social platforms, positioning itself as a calmer alternative for staying in touch with a small circle of people.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bebo is free for individual members. There is no subscription fee to create or join a Crew, and no premium tier has been publicized as part of the current relaunch.

Because Bebo has gone through several relaunches and shutdowns since regaining independence in 2013, prospective users should treat it as an independent, founder-funded project rather than a mainstream platform with guaranteed long-term continuity, and should check bebo.com directly for the most current feature set and availability.

faqs.question: Is Bebo still around in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bebo has been relaunched by its original founders, Michael and Xochi Birch, as a free group chat app built around private Crews.

faqs.question: Who owns Bebo now?
faqs.answer: Bebo is owned and operated by its original co-founders, Michael and Xochi Birch, who bought the brand back in 2013.

faqs.question: Is Bebo free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bebo is free for individual members with no subscription fee to create or join a Crew.

faqs.question: What happened to the original Bebo social network?
faqs.answer: The original Bebo, launched in 2005, was sold to AOL in 2008 for roughly 850 million US dollars, then sold off to Criterion Capital Partners in 2010 before the Birches repurchased it in 2013.

faqs.question: What is a Crew in Bebo?
faqs.answer: A Crew is a private group chat of up to about 100 people, functioning as the core social unit of the current Bebo app.

faqs.question: What is the Spectator Model?
faqs.answer: The Spectator Model lets a Crew go semi-public so outside viewers can watch a live conversation among core members without participating unless invited.

faqs.question: Has Bebo shut down before?
faqs.answer: Yes, prior relaunches, including a mobile social and livestreaming app around 2017, and a later private-beta version, were both discontinued before the current relaunch.

faqs.question: Is Bebo available as a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Access options have varied across Bebo's relaunches, so check bebo.com directly for the current availability of web or mobile access.

enrichment.short_description: Bedrock Analytics is a cloud-based analytics platform that harmonizes syndicated and retail sales data into AI-assisted dashboards and reporting for consumer packaged goods brands, including the cannabis industry.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing based on data volume; contact sales (extra users from $50 to $100 per month each)
enrichment.seo_title: Bedrock Analytics: CPG and Cannabis Data Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bedrock Analytics harmonizes retail and syndicated data into AI-powered dashboards for CPG and cannabis brands. See features and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bedrock Analytics, accessible at bedrockanalytics.com, is a cloud-based consumer packaged goods (CPG) analytics platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in the San Francisco Bay Area (Oakland, California). The company was built by a team combining CPG industry experts and Silicon Valley technologists to help manufacturers and brands extract more value from retail sales data. Bedrock harmonizes data from major syndicators such as Nielsen, Circana/IRI, SPINS, and NPD, alongside retailer point-of-sale, panel, and e-commerce data, into a single platform. The platform covers more than 350 product categories, giving it particular strength in specialized or emerging categories such as cannabis, where standardized retail data has historically been harder to access; Bedrock is frequently associated with cannabis and CPG retail analytics for this reason, though its stated positioning is a general CPG analytics platform rather than a cannabis-exclusive tool. Core platform capabilities include over 30 CPG-specific data visualizations, customizable dashboards, promotion and pricing analysis, and automated sales deck generation, aimed at replacing manual spreadsheet work for category managers and sales teams. Bedrock has incorporated a multi-model AI stack, referencing integration with models such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Llama, to power natural-language querying and AI-assisted storytelling from retail data. Pricing is not published; Bedrock uses a variable, quote-based pricing model tied to five factors: the number of product categories in scope, the number of data providers and syndicators connected, the number of retail portals integrated, the number of user accounts, and the level of market segmentation required (20, 50, or 100 aggregate segments). Ten user accounts are included by default, with additional users billed at roughly 100 US dollars per month each up to 20 users, and 50 US dollars per month per user beyond that. All plans include access to a support team described as more than 15 category managers with live chat support. Bedrock Analytics is venture-backed and positions itself against legacy data providers by emphasizing self-service visualization and AI-driven insight generation rather than raw data delivery alone. It is best suited for CPG brand analytics, sales, and category management teams that need to unify multiple retail data sources into one reporting layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bedrock Analytics
paragraphs: Bedrock Analytics is a cloud-based consumer packaged goods (CPG) analytics platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in Oakland, California, in the San Francisco Bay Area. The company was built by a team combining CPG industry veterans and Silicon Valley technologists to help brands and manufacturers extract more value from retail sales data.

The platform harmonizes data from major syndicators, including Nielsen, Circana/IRI, SPINS, and NPD, together with retailer point-of-sale, panel, and e-commerce data, into a single reporting layer. Bedrock covers more than 350 product categories, and it has become particularly associated with the cannabis industry, where standardized retail data has historically been harder to access than in more established CPG categories, though the platform is positioned as a general CPG analytics solution rather than a cannabis-only tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include more than 30 CPG-specific data visualizations, customizable dashboards, promotion and pricing analysis, and automated sales deck generation intended to replace manual spreadsheet work for category managers and sales teams.

Bedrock has layered in a multi-model AI stack, referencing integration with models such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Llama, to support natural-language querying and AI-assisted narrative generation from retail data, alongside access to a support team described as more than 15 in-house category managers offering live chat support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bedrock Analytics does not publish fixed subscription tiers. Instead, pricing is quote-based and tied to five factors: the number of product categories in scope, the number of connected data providers and syndicators, the number of integrated retail portals, the number of user accounts, and the chosen level of market segmentation (20, 50, or 100 aggregate segments).

Ten user accounts are included by default, with additional users billed at roughly 100 US dollars per month each up to 20 total users, and 50 US dollars per month per user beyond that. Prospective customers need to contact Bedrock directly through its website to receive a personalized quote.

faqs.question: What is Bedrock Analytics used for?
faqs.answer: Bedrock Analytics is used by CPG brands and manufacturers to harmonize retail and syndicated sales data into dashboards, visualizations, and AI-assisted reporting.

faqs.question: Does Bedrock Analytics only serve the cannabis industry?
faqs.answer: No. Bedrock is positioned as a general CPG analytics platform covering 350+ categories, though it has strong recognition and coverage within the cannabis industry.

faqs.question: What data sources does Bedrock Analytics integrate?
faqs.answer: It integrates syndicated data providers such as Nielsen, Circana/IRI, SPINS, and NPD, along with retailer point-of-sale, panel, and e-commerce data.

faqs.question: How much does Bedrock Analytics cost?
faqs.answer: Bedrock does not publish fixed pricing; cost is based on categories, data providers, retail portals, users, and segmentation level, with quotes provided directly by Bedrock's sales team.

faqs.question: Does Bedrock Analytics use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform integrates a multi-model AI stack referencing ChatGPT, Claude, and Llama for insight generation and reporting.

faqs.question: Who is Bedrock Analytics for?
faqs.answer: It is designed for CPG brand analytics, sales, and category management teams that need a unified view of retail sales data.

faqs.answer: Bedrock Analytics is headquartered in Oakland, California, in the San Francisco Bay Area.

faqs.question: How many product categories does Bedrock Analytics cover?
faqs.answer: Bedrock covers more than 350 product categories across CPG and cannabis retail data.

enrichment.short_description: Beezwax Datatools is a Claris FileMaker development and custom software consultancy that builds data, web, mobile, and analytics solutions, and maintains its own FileMaker tools and plugins.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom quotes based on project scope; no published rates
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco Bay Area, California, US
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Beezwax Datatools is a Claris Platinum Partner offering custom FileMaker, web, and mobile development since 1996. See services and expertise.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Beezwax Datatools, Inc., found at beezwax.net, is a custom software development and data consultancy that has operated since 1996, making it one of the longer-running independent firms in the Claris FileMaker ecosystem. The company is headquartered in the San Francisco Bay Area and works with clients throughout the United States and internationally. Beezwax describes its work as spanning more than 2,000 custom data, web, mobile, and analytics projects completed for over 600 clients across its history. Its primary specialization is Claris FileMaker application development, and Beezwax holds Claris Platinum Partner status, the highest partner tier in the Claris/FileMaker ecosystem, along with Claris Excellence Awards for Advocacy and Lifetime Achievement; longtime team member Vincenzo (Vince) Menanno has been recognized for more than 30 years of FileMaker community leadership. Beyond FileMaker, Beezwax's engineering team also works in JavaScript, Ruby on Rails, and iOS application development, positioning the company as a full-stack partner for custom business software rather than a single-technology shop. In addition to client services, Beezwax builds and maintains its own products and plugins for the FileMaker platform, which it distributes alongside its consulting practice. The company operates as a distributed team and is known within the Claris developer community for its blog and conference presence, including talks at FileMaker and Claris industry events. Because Beezwax is fundamentally a services and consulting business rather than a self-serve SaaS product, it does not publish fixed subscription pricing; engagements are quoted per project based on scope, timeline, and the mix of custom development, data migration, or ongoing support required. Beezwax is best suited for organizations that already run on FileMaker/Claris and need custom app development, data integration, or modernization work, as well as businesses evaluating a Claris Platinum Partner for new custom software builds outside the FileMaker ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Beezwax Datatools
paragraphs: Beezwax Datatools, Inc. is a custom software development and data consultancy headquartered in the San Francisco Bay Area that has operated since 1996, making it one of the longer-running independent firms in the Claris FileMaker ecosystem. The company works with clients throughout the United States and internationally through a distributed team.

Across its history, Beezwax reports completing more than 2,000 custom data, web, mobile, and analytics projects for over 600 clients, with its core specialization in Claris FileMaker application development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beezwax holds Claris Platinum Partner status, the top partner tier in the Claris/FileMaker ecosystem, and has received Claris Excellence Awards for both Advocacy and Lifetime Achievement; longtime team member Vincenzo (Vince) Menanno has been recognized for more than 30 years of FileMaker community leadership.

Beyond FileMaker, Beezwax's team also works in JavaScript, Ruby on Rails, and iOS app development, and the company builds and maintains its own proprietary plugins and tools for the FileMaker platform, which it distributes alongside its client consulting practice.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Beezwax is fundamentally a services and consulting business rather than a self-serve SaaS product, so it does not publish fixed subscription pricing. Engagements are quoted per project based on scope, timeline, and the mix of custom development, data migration, or ongoing support required, and prospective clients need to contact Beezwax directly for an estimate.

enrichment.short_description: Befonts is a font discovery website offering thousands of free and premium fonts curated for designers, with clear guidance on personal versus commercial licensing.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for personal-use fonts; premium and commercial fonts are priced individually by their creators
enrichment.seo_title: Befonts: Free and Premium Fonts for Designers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Befonts curates thousands of free and premium fonts for designers, with clear personal-use and commercial-use licensing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Befonts, at befonts.com, is a font discovery and download website that curates typefaces for designers, offering a mix of free and premium fonts sourced from independent type designers. The site organizes its catalog by category, including serif, sans-serif, script, display, brush, and grotesque styles, and features dedicated sections such as Top Fonts, Free Fonts for Personal Use, Free Fonts for Commercial Use, and a directory of font designers whose work is featured on the platform. Befonts positions itself as a curation layer rather than a font foundry itself: the fonts listed are created and licensed by third-party designers, and Befonts surfaces and organizes them for visitors. Licensing on Befonts follows a two-tier model common to font marketplaces: many fonts are free for personal, non-commercial use only, while a separate, clearly labeled set of fonts is free for commercial use without additional licensing required for desktop use in a business setting; fonts intended for logo design, advertising, or products sold commercially generally require the buyer to purchase or verify a commercial license from the original designer. Because individual fonts are supplied by many different independent creators, licensing terms, and by extension pricing for premium or commercial licenses, vary font by font rather than following a single site-wide price list. Befonts has been used by a large volume of designers over the years and is commonly referenced alongside other font resource sites such as Google Fonts, DaFont, and Font Squirrel as a place to browse new and trending typefaces. The site does not publish detailed corporate information such as a founding date, headquarters, or team size, consistent with its identity as a content and curation website rather than a company with an enterprise sales presence. Befonts is best suited for graphic designers, web designers, and hobbyists looking to browse, preview, and download fonts for personal projects, with the option to pursue a commercial license for fonts used in paid or branded work.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Befonts
paragraphs: Befonts, at befonts.com, is a font discovery and download website that curates typefaces for designers, combining free and premium fonts sourced from independent type designers rather than producing fonts itself.

The site organizes its catalog into categories such as serif, sans-serif, script, display, brush, and grotesque styles, and includes dedicated sections like Top Fonts, Free Fonts for Personal Use, Free Fonts for Commercial Use, and a directory profiling the individual font designers featured on the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Licensing on Befonts follows a two-tier model common to font marketplaces: many fonts are free for personal, non-commercial use only, while a separate, clearly labeled set of fonts is free for commercial use without additional licensing required for standard desktop use in a business setting.

Fonts intended for logo design, advertising, or products sold commercially generally require purchasing or verifying a commercial license from the original designer. Because many independent creators supply fonts to the site, licensing terms, and pricing for premium or commercial licenses, vary font by font rather than following one site-wide price list.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Befonts itself does not charge a platform-wide subscription fee. Personal-use fonts are free to download, while commercial and premium fonts are priced individually by their respective designers, and buyers purchase or license those fonts directly through the relevant font page.

Some fonts require a separate purchase for commercial use

faqs.question: Is Befonts free?
faqs.answer: Many fonts on Befonts are free for personal use, while other fonts are premium or require a commercial license.

faqs.question: Can I use Befonts fonts commercially?
faqs.answer: It depends on the specific font; Befonts labels fonts that are free for commercial use separately from personal-use-only fonts, and some premium fonts require a purchased license.

faqs.question: Who makes the fonts on Befonts?
faqs.answer: The fonts are created by independent, third-party type designers whose work Befonts curates and features.

faqs.question: What is Befonts?
faqs.answer: Befonts is a website that curates and offers free and premium fonts for designers to browse and download.

faqs.question: What types of fonts does Befonts offer?
faqs.answer: Befonts organizes fonts by style, including serif, sans-serif, script, display, brush, and grotesque typefaces.

faqs.question: How is Befonts different from a type foundry?
faqs.answer: Befonts does not design fonts itself; it curates and distributes fonts created by independent designers.

faqs.question: Is Befonts good for logo design fonts?
faqs.answer: Fonts used in logo design or commercial products generally require a commercial license, which Befonts labels separately from personal-use fonts.

faqs.question: Does Befonts charge for premium fonts?
faqs.answer: Yes, premium and commercial fonts are priced individually by their designers rather than through a single site-wide fee.

enrichment.seo_title: Benchmark: The VC Firm Behind eBay, Uber, and Twitter
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Benchmark is a Silicon Valley VC firm founded in 1995, known for early bets on eBay, Twitter, Uber, and Snapchat. See its history and portfolio.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Benchmark, legally Benchmark Capital Advisors LLC and commonly known simply as Benchmark, is a venture capital firm founded in 1995 by Bob Kagle, Bruce Dunlevie, Andy Rachleff, Kevin Harvey, and Val Vaden. The firm is headquartered in San Francisco, with historical roots in nearby Woodside, California, and focuses on early-stage (seed and Series A) investing rather than growth-stage or late-stage rounds. Benchmark is widely regarded as one of the most successful and storied venture firms in Silicon Valley history. Its most famous early investment was a 6.7 million US dollar check for 22.1 percent of eBay in 1997, a stake that eventually returned more than 5 billion US dollars and helped establish Benchmark's reputation. Since then, Benchmark has been an early investor in a long list of category-defining companies, including Twitter, Instagram, Uber, Snapchat, Yelp, OpenTable, Zendesk, Zillow, New Relic, Elastic, Stitch Fix, Discord, and Juniper Networks, among many others. From 2011 onward, the firm counted more than 35 exits, including over a dozen IPOs and more than 20 mergers and acquisitions, representing tens of billions of dollars in realized value. Structurally, Benchmark is distinctive for operating as a flat, equal partnership: all general partners share ownership and compensation equally, with no managing partner, no senior-junior hierarchy, and no firm name built around a single founder, a deliberate departure from how most VC firms are organized. As of the mid-2020s, Benchmark's general partners include figures such as Peter Fenton, Eric Vishria, Sarah Tavel, Chetan Puttagunta, and Victor Lazarte, with the firm continuing to raise concentrated, relatively small funds by industry standards in order to preserve high per-partner ownership stakes in portfolio companies. Because Benchmark is a private investment partnership rather than a software product or service, it has no subscription pricing, no free trial, and no software features in the conventional sense; the firm's core activity is sourcing, evaluating, and funding early-stage startups in exchange for equity, then supporting portfolio companies through board seats and operational guidance. Benchmark does not publicly disclose fund sizes or assets under management with full consistency, though third-party estimates place total assets under management in the single-digit billions of US dollars, reflecting its strategy of staying deliberately small relative to peer mega-funds.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Benchmark
paragraphs: Benchmark, legally Benchmark Capital Advisors LLC, is a venture capital firm founded in 1995 by Bob Kagle, Bruce Dunlevie, Andy Rachleff, Kevin Harvey, and Val Vaden. The firm is headquartered in San Francisco, with historical roots in nearby Woodside, California, and focuses on early-stage investing, primarily seed and Series A rounds.

Benchmark is widely regarded as one of the most successful venture firms in Silicon Valley history. Its most famous early investment was a 6.7 million US dollar check for 22.1 percent of eBay in 1997, a stake that eventually returned more than 5 billion US dollars and helped establish the firm's reputation.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Benchmark Invests
paragraphs: Since its eBay investment, Benchmark has been an early backer of a long list of category-defining companies, including Twitter, Instagram, Uber, Snapchat, Yelp, OpenTable, Zendesk, Zillow, New Relic, Elastic, Stitch Fix, and Discord, among many others. From 2011 onward, the firm recorded more than 35 exits, including over a dozen IPOs and more than 20 mergers and acquisitions.

Structurally, Benchmark is distinctive for operating as a flat, equal partnership: all general partners share ownership and compensation equally, with no managing partner, no senior-junior hierarchy, and no associate or junior-investor layer, a deliberate departure from how most VC firms are organized. As of the mid-2020s, Benchmark's general partners include Peter Fenton, Eric Vishria, Sarah Tavel, Chetan Puttagunta, and Victor Lazarte.

long_form_blocks.heading: Fund Structure
paragraphs: Benchmark is a private investment partnership, not a software product, so it has no subscription pricing, free trial, or purchasable plan. Instead, Benchmark invests its own managed funds directly into startups in exchange for equity, typically taking a board seat and providing hands-on operational support to portfolio companies.

The firm deliberately raises smaller, more concentrated funds than many peer mega-funds in order to preserve high per-partner ownership stakes in each portfolio company; third-party estimates place its total assets under management in the single-digit billions of US dollars, though Benchmark does not consistently disclose exact fund sizes.

features.title: Equal-partnership investment model
features.description: All general partners share ownership and compensation equally, with no hierarchy of senior or junior partners.

features.title: Early-stage focus
features.description: Concentrates primarily on seed and Series A investments rather than growth or late-stage rounds.

features.title: Board-level involvement
features.description: General partners typically take board seats and provide hands-on support to founders.

features.title: Concentrated portfolio strategy
features.description: Makes fewer, larger bets rather than spreading investment across a very large number of companies.

features.title: Sector-agnostic technology investing
features.description: Invests across consumer, enterprise, and marketplace technology companies.

features.title: Track record of major exits
features.description: Backed companies including eBay, Twitter, Uber, Instagram, and Snapchat through to IPO or acquisition.

features.title: No associate or junior-investor layer
features.description: Deal sourcing and evaluation are handled directly by general partners rather than junior staff.

features.title: Deliberately small fund sizes
features.description: Raises smaller funds relative to peers to preserve high ownership stakes per portfolio company.

pros: Highly selective, high-signal backing for portfolio founders
Hands-on senior partner involvement with no associate layer
Track record including massive exits like eBay, Uber, and Twitter
Flat, equal-partner structure that aligns partner incentives
Concentrated portfolio allows deeper support per company
cons: Extremely selective, making it difficult for most startups to get funded
Primarily deal-flow driven through warm introductions rather than open applications
Does not run large-scale growth or late-stage follow-on investing like mega-funds
Small firm size limits the number of companies it can actively support at once

faqs.question: What is Benchmark?
faqs.answer: Benchmark is a Silicon Valley venture capital firm founded in 1995 that invests primarily in early-stage technology startups.

faqs.question: What stage does Benchmark invest in?
faqs.answer: Benchmark focuses primarily on seed and Series A stage investments.

faqs.question: What companies has Benchmark backed?
faqs.answer: Notable Benchmark investments include eBay, Twitter, Instagram, Uber, Snapchat, Yelp, OpenTable, and Zendesk, among many others.

faqs.question: Who founded Benchmark?
faqs.answer: Benchmark was founded in 1995 by Bob Kagle, Bruce Dunlevie, Andy Rachleff, Kevin Harvey, and Val Vaden.

faqs.question: What makes Benchmark's partnership structure unique?
faqs.answer: Benchmark operates as a flat, equal partnership where all general partners share ownership and compensation equally, without a managing partner or junior-investor hierarchy.

faqs.question: How much does Benchmark manage?
faqs.answer: Benchmark does not consistently disclose exact fund sizes, though third-party estimates place its assets under management in the single-digit billions of US dollars.

faqs.question: Where is Benchmark based?
faqs.answer: Benchmark is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with historical roots in Woodside, California.

faqs.question: How do founders get funding from Benchmark?
faqs.answer: Benchmark typically evaluates and funds startups through direct relationships and warm introductions to its general partners rather than an open application process.

enrichment.short_description: Benevity is an enterprise corporate purpose platform that unifies employee giving, volunteering, grants management, and impact reporting for large employers.
enrichment.seo_title: Benevity: Corporate Giving and Volunteering Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Benevity is an enterprise CSR platform unifying employee giving, volunteering, and grants management. See features, pricing, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Benevity is an enterprise software platform for corporate social responsibility (CSR), employee giving, and workplace volunteering, founded in Calgary, Alberta, Canada in 2008 by Bryan de Lottinville. The company, marketed as the Benevity Enterprise Impact Platform, brings together employee giving, volunteering, grants and scholarship management, employee resource groups, engagement challenges, and impact reporting in a single system aimed at large employers. Benevity's core value proposition is consolidating what would otherwise be several disconnected CSR and engagement tools, such as matching-gift programs, volunteer time tracking, disaster relief campaigns, and nonprofit vetting, into one platform that integrates with a company's existing HR and payroll systems. The platform supports programs in more than 20 languages and connects employees to a large, pre-vetted network of nonprofit organizations worldwide, handling compliance and disbursement so companies do not need to manage individual donation payouts themselves. As of the mid-2020s, Benevity reports having processed more than 10 billion US dollars in donations and facilitated more than 46 million hours of tracked employee volunteering, supporting more than 326,000 nonprofit organizations, and has awarded more than 1.2 million grants through its platform. Benevity's customer base skews toward large, brand-name enterprises, and the company positions itself as a leading corporate purpose software provider, competing with platforms in the employee-giving and CSR space. Benevity does not publish self-serve or tiered subscription pricing; access is sold through custom enterprise contracts negotiated directly with the company, with pricing generally scaled to the size of the client organization and the specific mix of giving, volunteering, and grants modules deployed. Because implementations typically involve integration with a client's HR systems, payroll, and internal communications tools, onboarding is handled through Benevity's sales and customer success teams rather than a self-service signup flow. The company employs roughly 750 to 850 people globally as of the mid-2020s and operates from multiple offices in addition to its Calgary headquarters. Benevity is best suited for mid-size to large enterprises building or scaling a structured corporate social responsibility, employee giving, or volunteering program.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Benevity
paragraphs: Benevity is an enterprise software platform for corporate social responsibility (CSR), employee giving, and workplace volunteering, founded in Calgary, Alberta, Canada in 2008 by Bryan de Lottinville. The company markets its product as the Benevity Enterprise Impact Platform.

The platform brings together employee giving, volunteering, grants and scholarship management, employee resource groups, engagement challenges, and impact reporting in a single system aimed at large employers, consolidating tools that would otherwise be spread across multiple disconnected vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Benevity supports programs in more than 20 languages and connects employees to a large, pre-vetted network of nonprofit organizations, handling compliance and payout disbursement so companies do not need to manage individual donations themselves. As of the mid-2020s, Benevity reports having processed more than 10 billion US dollars in donations and facilitated over 46 million hours of tracked employee volunteering across more than 326,000 nonprofit organizations, with more than 1.2 million grants awarded through the platform.

Because implementations typically integrate with a client's HR systems, payroll, and internal communications tools, Benevity's customer base skews toward mid-size to large, brand-name enterprises, and onboarding is handled through the company's sales and customer success teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Benevity does not publish self-serve or tiered subscription pricing. Access is sold through custom enterprise contracts negotiated directly with the company, generally scaled to organization size and the specific mix of giving, volunteering, and grants modules deployed, with prospective customers required to request a demo and quote.

features.description: Connects employees to a pre-vetted network of more than 326,000 nonprofit organizations worldwide.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Supports corporate giving and volunteering programs in more than 20 languages.

pros: Consolidates multiple CSR programs into a single platform
Large, pre-vetted nonprofit network reduces compliance burden
Strong reporting and analytics for ESG disclosures
Trusted by many large, brand-name enterprises
Supports global, multi-language workforces
cons: Enterprise-only pricing not accessible to small businesses
Pricing is not published and requires a sales engagement
Implementation requires HR and payroll integration work
May be more platform than smaller organizations need

faqs.question: What is Benevity used for?
faqs.answer: Benevity is used by enterprises to manage employee giving, volunteering, grants, and corporate social responsibility reporting in one platform.

faqs.question: Who founded Benevity and when?
faqs.answer: Benevity was founded in Calgary, Alberta, Canada in 2008 by Bryan de Lottinville.

faqs.question: How much does Benevity cost?
faqs.answer: Benevity does not publish fixed pricing; it sells custom enterprise contracts scaled to organization size and modules used.

faqs.question: What is the Benevity Enterprise Impact Platform?
faqs.answer: It is Benevity's unified product covering employee giving, volunteering, grants management, employee resource groups, and impact reporting.

faqs.question: Does Benevity support corporate volunteering?
faqs.answer: Yes, Benevity includes tools for tracking and reporting employee volunteer hours.

faqs.question: How many nonprofits are on Benevity's network?
faqs.answer: Benevity connects to a pre-vetted network of more than 326,000 nonprofit organizations worldwide.

faqs.question: Is Benevity only for large companies?
faqs.answer: Benevity's enterprise pricing and implementation model make it best suited for mid-size to large organizations rather than very small businesses.

faqs.question: Where is Benevity headquartered?
faqs.answer: Benevity is headquartered in Calgary, Alberta, Canada.

enrichment.short_description: Better Uptime was rebranded to Better Stack in 2023; its uptime monitoring product now operates within the Better Stack observability platform alongside logs, metrics, and incident management.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available for 10 monitors; paid uptime plans start around $29 per month
enrichment.seo_title: Better Uptime is Now Better Stack: What Changed
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Better Uptime rebranded to Better Stack in 2023. See what its uptime monitoring, incident management, and status pages offer today.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Better Uptime was the original name of a website, server, and API uptime monitoring product built by the company now known as Better Stack, headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic. The product first launched around 2020 as a Heroku add-on and standalone SaaS offering checks, incident alerting with phone call escalation, on-call scheduling, and hosted status pages. In 2023, the company rebranded its full product suite from Better Uptime and a companion log-management tool called Logtail to a unified platform called Better Stack, reflecting the company's expansion beyond uptime checks into logs, metrics, traces, dashboards, and broader incident management. Under the new branding, what used to be called Better Uptime is now marketed simply as Uptime, one module within the Better Stack platform, reachable at betterstack.com/uptime; the betteruptime.com domain and branding now point to this Better Stack product rather than a separate standalone service. Functionally, the uptime monitoring module checks websites, servers, APIs, SSL certificates, and heartbeats from monitoring locations on multiple continents, with check intervals as frequent as 30 seconds on paid plans and 3 minutes on the free plan. It includes incident management with phone, SMS, Slack, and Microsoft Teams alerting, on-call scheduling with escalation policies, Playwright-based synthetic transaction monitoring, and customizable public status pages. Better Stack offers a free plan covering up to 10 monitors and heartbeats, one status page, and basic Slack and email alerting, while paid plans begin at approximately 29 US dollars per month and add unlimited team members, phone and SMS alerts, faster check intervals, on-call escalation, SSO, SCIM, role-based access control, and audit logging; the company also advertises a 60-day money-back guarantee on paid plans. Better Stack integrates with common cloud and observability tools including AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, Datadog, New Relic, Grafana, Prometheus, PagerDuty, and Opsgenie. Because the Better Uptime name persists in search traffic, documentation history, and third-party integrations such as its Heroku add-on listing, it is commonly treated as the predecessor brand or synonym for Better Stack's uptime monitoring product rather than a separate, currently marketed product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Better Uptime (Better Stack)
paragraphs: Better Uptime was the original name of a website, server, and API uptime monitoring product built by the company now known as Better Stack, headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic. The product launched around 2020, initially gaining traction partly through a Heroku add-on listing, and offered uptime checks, incident alerting with phone-call escalation, on-call scheduling, and hosted status pages.

In 2023, the company rebranded its full product suite, including Better Uptime and a companion log-management tool called Logtail, into a unified platform called Better Stack, reflecting its expansion beyond uptime checks into logs, metrics, traces, dashboards, and broader incident management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Under the current branding, what used to be marketed as Better Uptime is now the Uptime module within Better Stack, reachable at betterstack.com/uptime; the betteruptime.com brand still commonly refers to this same product. It checks websites, servers, APIs, SSL certificates, and heartbeats from monitoring locations across multiple continents, with check intervals as fast as 30 seconds on paid plans and 3 minutes on the free plan.

The product includes incident management with phone, SMS, Slack, and Microsoft Teams alerting, on-call scheduling with escalation policies, Playwright-based synthetic transaction monitoring, and customizable public status pages, plus integrations with tools such as AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, Datadog, New Relic, Grafana, Prometheus, PagerDuty, and Opsgenie.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Better Stack offers a free plan covering up to 10 monitors and heartbeats, one status page, and basic Slack and email alerting. Paid plans begin at roughly 29 US dollars per month and add unlimited team members, phone and SMS alerts, faster 30-second check intervals, on-call escalation, and Playwright-based transaction checks.

Higher enterprise tiers add SSO, SCIM, role-based access control, audit logging, and advanced reporting, typically at custom pricing. The company also advertises a 60-day money-back guarantee on its paid plans.

features.title: Website, server, and API uptime monitoring
features.description: Checks endpoints from multiple global regions with intervals as fast as 30 seconds on paid plans.

features.title: Incident management and alerting

features.title: Hosted, customizable status pages
features.description: Public status pages that can be branded and customized per company.

features.title: Synthetic transaction monitoring
features.description: Uses Playwright-based scripted checks to test multi-step user flows.

features.title: SSL certificate and heartbeat monitoring
features.description: Monitors certificate expiry and scheduled/cron job heartbeats.

features.title: Multi-region checks
features.description: Runs checks from monitoring locations across multiple continents.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connects with AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, Datadog, Grafana, PagerDuty, and other tools.

pros: Generous free tier covering 10 monitors
Fast 30-second check intervals on paid plans
Combines uptime monitoring with broader observability under one account
Reliable phone-call incident alerting
Well-regarded, customizable status pages
cons: Full observability suite (logs and metrics) is priced separately from uptime monitoring
Brand confusion persists from the Better Uptime to Better Stack rebrand
Enterprise features like SSO, SCIM, and RBAC require higher-tier plans
Some advanced monitoring features are gated behind paid tiers

faqs.question: Is Better Uptime still a separate product?
faqs.answer: No. Better Uptime was rebranded to Better Stack in 2023, and its functionality now lives as the Uptime module within the Better Stack platform.

faqs.question: What happened to Better Uptime?
faqs.answer: Better Uptime, along with the Logtail log-management tool, was rebranded and merged into the unified Better Stack platform in 2023.

faqs.question: Is Better Stack free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Better Stack offers a free plan covering up to 10 monitors and heartbeats, one status page, and basic alerting.

faqs.question: How much does Better Stack's uptime monitoring cost?
faqs.answer: Paid uptime monitoring plans start at approximately 29 US dollars per month, with enterprise tiers priced separately.

faqs.question: Does Better Uptime still work as a Heroku add-on?
faqs.answer: The Better Uptime name is still associated with a legacy Heroku add-on listing, which now maps to Better Stack's uptime monitoring product.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Better Uptime and Better Stack?
faqs.answer: They are the same underlying company and product line; Better Uptime was the original brand, and Better Stack is the current, expanded branding covering uptime, logs, metrics, and incident management.

faqs.question: Where is Better Stack headquartered?
faqs.answer: Better Stack is headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic.

faqs.question: What features does Better Stack's uptime monitoring include?
faqs.answer: It includes multi-region uptime checks, incident alerting, on-call scheduling, synthetic transaction monitoring, and hosted status pages.

enrichment.short_description: Betterbird is a free, open-source, donation-funded fork of Mozilla Thunderbird that closely tracks Thunderbird's ESR releases while adding extra features and faster bug fixes.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Betterbird is a free, donation-funded fork of Mozilla Thunderbird with extra features and faster bug fixes. See what it offers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Betterbird, available at betterbird.eu, is a free and open-source email client built as a soft fork of Mozilla Thunderbird. The project is led by Jorg Knobloch, a longtime Thunderbird contributor who served as Thunderbird's first paid employee and its maintainer and release engineer from roughly 2016 to 2019, contributing more than 2,000 commits to the official Thunderbird codebase before parting ways with the Thunderbird project over concerns about quality assurance and bug triage practices. Betterbird launched publicly around 2022 and has since released versions in parallel with Thunderbird's Extended Support Release (ESR) cycle, a deliberate strategy to avoid the fate of earlier Thunderbird forks that drifted from upstream and fell behind on security patches. Unlike a hard fork, Betterbird pulls its source directly from Mozilla's Thunderbird repositories and layers a public, transparent set of patches on top, published in a GitHub repository, with the stated goal of eventually contributing improvements back upstream to Thunderbird itself. Betterbird is distributed under the same MPL-2.0 open-source license as Thunderbird and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux. Because it tracks the same profile format as Thunderbird, users can switch between Thunderbird and Betterbird on the same profile and version without conversion, making it a low-risk trial for existing Thunderbird users. Notable features added on top of stock Thunderbird include a multi-line message list view, an improved system tray tooltip on Linux showing new messages by folder, more powerful search with support for excluding criteria and regular expressions, and a feature that remembers recently used attachments so files can be reattached without re-browsing folders. Betterbird has no purchase price and carries no subscription fee; the project is entirely funded by voluntary donations processed through Stripe, PayPal, and regional bank transfer options across North America, Europe, the UK, Canada, and Australia and New Zealand. Reports indicate fewer than 2 percent of Betterbird's users financially support the project, with roughly half of donations coming from North America and half from Europe, particularly Germany. The project's fiscal residence is in Spain. Betterbird is best suited for existing Thunderbird users who want faster bug fixes, additional power-user features, and closer adherence to Thunderbird's official release cadence without waiting on Mozilla's own release and QA timeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Betterbird
paragraphs: Betterbird, available at betterbird.eu, is a free and open-source email client built as a soft fork of Mozilla Thunderbird. The project is led by Jorg Knobloch, a longtime Thunderbird contributor who served as Thunderbird's first paid employee and its maintainer and release engineer from roughly 2016 to 2019, contributing more than 2,000 commits to the official Thunderbird codebase.

Betterbird launched publicly around 2022 after Knobloch parted ways with the official Thunderbird project over concerns about quality assurance and bug-triage practices, and has since released versions in parallel with Thunderbird's Extended Support Release (ESR) cycle.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Unlike a hard fork, Betterbird pulls its source directly from Mozilla's Thunderbird repositories and layers a public, transparent set of patches on top, published in a GitHub repository, with the stated goal of eventually contributing improvements back upstream to Thunderbird itself. It is distributed under the same MPL-2.0 license as Thunderbird and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, sharing the same profile format so users can switch between the two clients on the same version without conversion.

Notable features layered on top of stock Thunderbird include a multi-line message list view, an improved system tray tooltip on Linux that shows new messages by folder, more powerful search with support for excluding criteria and regular expressions, and a feature that remembers recently used attachments so files can be reattached without re-browsing folders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Betterbird has no purchase price and no subscription fee; the project is entirely funded by voluntary donations processed through Stripe, PayPal, and regional bank transfer options across North America, Europe, the UK, Canada, and Australia and New Zealand. Reports indicate fewer than 2 percent of Betterbird's users financially support the project, with donations split roughly evenly between North America and Europe, particularly Germany.

features.title: Multi-line message list view
features.description: Displays multiple lines of message information in the list view, a long-requested Thunderbird feature.

features.title: Enhanced system tray tooltips
features.description: Shows new messages by folder in the system tray on Linux.

features.title: Advanced search
features.description: Supports regular expressions and exclusion criteria in complex mail searches.

features.title: Recently-used attachments memory
features.description: Lets users reattach recently used files without browsing folders again.

features.title: Close ESR tracking with faster fixes
features.description: Follows Thunderbird's Extended Support Release cycle while shipping bug fixes faster than upstream.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

features.title: Profile compatibility with Thunderbird
features.description: Uses the same profile format, allowing easy switching between Thunderbird and Betterbird.

features.title: Public, transparent patch repository
features.description: Publishes all applied patches openly on GitHub.

pros: Completely free with no paywalled features
Faster bug fixes than stock Thunderbird
Easy to trial since it shares Thunderbird profiles
Transparent, publicly reviewable patches
Maintained by a Thunderbird industry veteran
Stays close to upstream to avoid security drift
cons: Run mainly by a single lead maintainer, creating a bus-factor risk
Funded almost entirely by a small percentage of donating users
Smaller team may respond more slowly to some edge-case bugs
Not officially affiliated with or supported by Mozilla

faqs.question: Is Betterbird free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Betterbird is free and open-source, funded entirely through voluntary donations.

faqs.question: Who created Betterbird?
faqs.answer: Betterbird is led by Jorg Knobloch, a former Thunderbird maintainer and release engineer.

faqs.question: Is Betterbird safe and legitimate?
faqs.answer: Betterbird is a soft fork that sources code directly from Mozilla's Thunderbird repositories and publishes its patches publicly on GitHub, closely tracking Thunderbird's ESR releases.

faqs.question: Can I switch between Thunderbird and Betterbird easily?
faqs.answer: Yes, both clients share the same profile format, so users can switch between them on the same version without conversion.

faqs.question: What platforms does Betterbird support?
faqs.answer: Betterbird runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: Does Betterbird share code with Thunderbird?
faqs.answer: Yes, Betterbird's source is pulled directly from Mozilla's Thunderbird repositories, with additional patches layered on top.

faqs.question: Does Betterbird contribute changes back to Thunderbird?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project's stated goal is to submit its improvements upstream so Thunderbird itself can eventually benefit.

faqs.question: How is Betterbird funded?
faqs.answer: Betterbird is funded entirely by voluntary donations via Stripe, PayPal, and regional bank transfers.

use_cases.title: Power-user email management
use_cases.description: Desktop email users adopt Betterbird for features like multi-line view and advanced search not yet in stock Thunderbird.

use_cases.title: Faster bug-fix adoption
use_cases.description: Thunderbird users frustrated by slow official fixes switch to Betterbird for quicker patches.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious open-source email use
use_cases.description: Privacy-focused individuals and organizations choose Betterbird as an open-source desktop email client.
